fact: The Windscale reactors had no containment building — just a graphite core enclosed in a steel and concrete shell with chimneys venting to the open air.
source: Windscale fire, Wikipedia (citing official UK government reports and Penney Report, 1957)

fact: The fire was caused by a Wigner energy release (annealing) cycle that raised the graphite core temperature too high, igniting the graphite.
source: Lorna Arnold, "Windscale 1957: Anatomy of a Nuclear Accident" (2007, Oxford University Press)

fact: Uranium fuel cartridges ruptured during the fire, releasing radioactive contamination including plutonium-bearing smoke up the chimney stack.
source: UK National Archives, Penney Report on the Windscale Accident (1957, declassified 1988)

fact: The chimney filters (known as 'Cockcroft's Follies') trapped a significant portion of radioactive particulates and prevented far worse contamination of northern England.
source: Lorna Arnold, "Windscale 1957: Anatomy of a Nuclear Accident" (2007); also widely documented in BBC and UK government retrospectives

fact: The filters were nearly not installed — they were added at the last minute at the insistence of Sir John Cockcroft, over objections about cost and delays.
source: Lorna Arnold, "Windscale 1957: Anatomy of a Nuclear Accident" (2007, Oxford University Press); BBC documentary accounts

fact: The Windscale fire occurred on October 10, 1957, and is considered the worst nuclear accident in British history.
source: Widely documented; UK Health and Safety Executive historical records; Wikipedia
